candidate_methods: Dataset discovery and ingestion via dandi-api; NWB handling with pynwb and Neo
SpikeInterface for standardized spike-sorting pipelines and quality metrics if raw data require sorting (or use curated unit lists when available)
Unit classification into putative interneurons vs excitatory neurons using waveform features (peak-to-trough, trough-to-peak, spike width), firing rate, and clustering
Peri-perturbation PSTHs and baseline-normalized firing-rate analyses
Pairwise spike-time cross-correlation and shuffled/shift predictors to estimate functional coupling; compute distribution of correlation peak amplitudes and widths
Population coupling (Okun et al.) to quantify each cell's coupling to network firing
Joint PSTH (JPSTH) and trial-averaged cross-correlograms aligned to perturbation events
Generalized linear models (GLMs) with coupling filters to quantify directed spike influence between cells; include covariates for firing rate and behavioral state
Spectral analyses: spike–LFP coherence, phase-locking value (PLV), and changes in LFP power spectra pre/post perturbation
Surrogate analyses (spike jitter, trial shuffling) to control for firing-rate-driven correlations
Mixed-effects models to combine sessions/animals while accounting for nested structure
Visualization and reproducibility using Jupyter notebooks, containerization (Docker/Singularity), and public code repository

dataset_needs.rationale: Need NWB-style electrophysiology datasets with explicit interneuron suppression or perturbation epochs (optogenetic silencing of PV/SST interneurons or pharmacological suppression) recorded in awake animals. DANDI hosts many NWB datasets; plan to query DANDI via the dandi-api for recordings containing optogenetic stimulus channels and sorted units. Primary analyses require unit-resolved spike times, LFP, and perturbation timestamps for within-session comparisons.
variables: unit_metadata (putative cell type: interneuron/pyramidal or waveform features)
session metadata (subject id, date, sampling rates)

dataset_needs.rationale: Independent datasets (same brain region) without perturbation are needed to estimate baseline coupling distributions, to build null models, and to validate that observed coupling changes are not pipeline-specific. These can come from other DANDI entries or OpenNeuro NWB conversions; keep dataset_id null to allow selection of best-matching public datasets.
variables: spike_times (unit-resolved) from control sessions without perturbation

execution_notes: Search DANDI via dandi-api for NWB recordings with 'optogenetic', 'PV', 'SST', 'inhibition', 'laser' and localize sessions with unit-sorted spikes and LFP. Use pynwb/Neo to load data, SpikeInterface to re-run quality metrics or spike sorting if needed, and store all processed outputs as NWB derivatives. Classify units with standardized code (waveform+rate rules) and document thresholds. Compute coupling metrics with Elephant and custom routines: cross-correlograms with shift-predictor, jittered surrogates, population coupling, GLMs (pyglmnet/statsmodels), and spectral analyses with MNE or SciPy. Pre-register analysis decisions (e.g., bin sizes, surrogate methods), include sanity checks (artifact removal around light pulses), and provide a public GitHub repo with notebooks, Dockerfile, and links/IDs of used DANDI/Open datasets to ensure reproducibility. Aim for within-session paired comparisons and mixed-effects models across animals to report effect sizes and confidence intervals rather than only p-values.
hypotheses: Acute reduction of interneuron firing (optogenetic or pharmacological) decreases inhibitory control and increases pairwise spike-time correlations among excitatory neurons.
Interneuron dampening increases low-frequency spike–LFP coherence and broadens cross-correlation peaks, reflecting more synchronous population events.
Changes in spike coupling after interneuron dampening are cell-type- and state-dependent (e.g., stronger during quiescence than during movement) and are not explained solely by firing-rate changes.

planned_figures.purpose: Show timeline of perturbation (interneuron dampening), example raw traces (LFP), raster of spikes with interneuron labels, and indicate analysis pipeline (NWB -> spike sorting -> coupling metrics) to orient reader and demonstrate data quality.
planned_figures.title: Experimental/analysis schematic and example dataset

planned_figures.purpose: Population PSTHs and scatter of firing-rate changes for interneurons vs excitatory neurons to establish immediate effects of the perturbation and verify targeted dampening.
planned_figures.title: Effect of interneuron dampening on firing rates

planned_figures.purpose: Distribution plots (violin/histogram) of pairwise cross-correlation peak amplitudes and widths before vs during perturbation, stratified by excitatory–excitatory and excitatory–interneuron pairs, with statistical tests and rate-controlled surrogates.
planned_figures.title: Change in pairwise spike correlations

planned_figures.purpose: Population coupling metric per unit pre/post perturbation and change in population event rates (e.g., network bursts), showing how interneuron dampening alters each neuron’s participation in synchronous events.
planned_figures.title: Population coupling and network synchrony

planned_figures.purpose: Spectrograms or band-limited power comparisons and spike–LFP coherence changes pre vs during perturbation to link spike coupling changes to LFP oscillatory dynamics.
planned_figures.title: Spike–LFP coherence and spectral changes

planned_figures.purpose: Example fitted GLM coupling filters for representative cell pairs and summary statistics across pairs; compare model-predicted coupling changes vs observed cross-correlations and show surrogate controls.
planned_figures.title: GLM coupling filters and model controls

question: How does acute dampening of interneuron activity alter spike–spike coupling and population synchrony among excitatory neurons in awake behaving animals?
risks: Public datasets with the exact experimental manipulation (acute interneuron suppression with simultaneous well-isolated units and LFP) may be scarce; dataset selection could limit statistical power.
Unit-type labels may be missing or unreliable; waveform-based classification is imperfect and may confound interneuron identification.
Optogenetic perturbations can introduce electrical/artifact transients that bias spike detection and LFP measures unless carefully controlled/removed.
Changes in firing rate can create apparent correlation changes; disentangling rate effects from genuine coupling changes requires conservative surrogates and controls.
Heterogeneity across labs/protocols (recording rigs, preprocessing, spike-sorting pipelines) may complicate meta-analysis and require harmonization steps.
